If this is indeed the truth from You, then rain upon us stones from the sky, or bring us a painful punishment.”

This is remarkable in its audacity. They’re essentially saying, “We’re so convinced this isn’t from You that we’re willing to be destroyed if we’re wrong.” It’s a challenge thrown at Allah ﷻ Himself, made publicly to demonstrate their certainty that the Quran is not divine revelation.

Their demand is specific: either shower rocks upon them from the sky—a rain of destruction—or bring them another form of painful punishment. They present themselves as ready to face annihilation as proof of their conviction.

Ayah 33

وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنتَ فِيهِمْ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ responds to their challenge, but His response reveals His wisdom and mercy rather than immediate wrath. He acknowledges their demand—it was heard and recorded—but then provides the rationale for why their request wasn’t granted.

The first reason: وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَأَنتَ فِيهِمْ—“Allah would not punish them while you are in their midst.” The presence of the Messenger ﷺ among them in Makkah was itself a protection from immediate divine punishment. This was impossible while he remained there.

But significantly, this ayah is Madani—revealed after the migration to Madinah. The Messenger ﷺ is no longer in Makkah. The implication is clear: the floodgates for punishment are now opening. The mechanism through which that punishment would be delivered? The battlefield at Badr.

The second reason reveals even more mercy: وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ مُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَهُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ—“And Allah would not punish them while some of them were seeking forgiveness.” Even among those who rejected publicly, some individuals were making istighfar, at least occasionally. Some hearts still had doubts about their position, some consciences still stirred. There was hope of goodness left in that society. Allah ﷻ hadn’t closed the door on them yet.

Ayah 34

وَمَا لَهُمْ أَلَّا يُعَذِّبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنْ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Now the tone shifts. Having explained why punishment was delayed, Allah ﷻ establishes why it is, in fact, deserved: “And what is there for them that Allah should not punish them?” The question itself indicates that punishment is warranted.

The reason: وَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ—“While they obstruct from the Sacred Masjid.” They’re actively preventing people—both themselves and others—from accessing the Masjid al-Haram. They’ve positioned themselves as gatekeepers of a sanctuary that doesn’t belong to them.

Allah ﷻ makes this explicit: وَمَا كَانُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥٓ—“And they are not its protective guardians.” They have no legitimate authority over the Sacred House. إِنْ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ—“Its guardians are none but the people of taqwa”—those who are truly conscious and cautious of Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a sobering observation: وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ—“However, most of them do not know.” They’re ignorant of this reality, unaware that their claim to the Kaaba’s guardianship is illegitimate. They don’t understand that taqwa, not tribal lineage or political control, determines true authority over Allah’s House.

Ayah 35

وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ عِندَ ٱلْبَيْتِ إِلَّا مُكَآءً وَتَصْدِيَةً ۚ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ

This ayah exposes the true nature of the disbelievers’ worship at the Kaaba. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t even dignify what they do with the proper grammatical form for salah. The text says وَمَا كَانَ صَلَاتُهُمْ—using a form that indicates this wasn’t real salah anyway.

So what was their “prayer” around the House of Allah? إِلَّا مُكَآءً وَتَصْدِيَةً—“Nothing but whistling and clapping.” They would circle the Kaaba whistling, clapping, dancing, and singing. The word مُكَآءً refers to whistling and, by extension, wind instruments. The word تَصْدِيَةً refers to clapping and, by extension, percussion instruments like drums.

This practice mirrors what we find in many religious traditions throughout history—music as a central component of worship. Even today, numerous faiths incorporate orchestras, church bands, choirs, and various musical instruments into their prayer services. We’ve even entered an age of Islamic religious music industry, which raises important questions about how we’re meant to achieve spiritual consciousness.

Allah ﷻ already gave us the ultimate form of gaining spiritual connection: the Quran itself. When the ayat are recited properly by those who truly know how to recite them, there’s a natural rhythm and power to the recitation. Different surahs carry different tones—some are war cries, some are gentle reminders, some carry warnings with weight and gravity. Consider Surah Muhammad—when recited properly, it’s literally a battle cry that raises the hair on your skin.

The Quran’s recitation has an intrinsic beauty and power that’s being lost when it’s reduced to merely checking someone’s tajweed accuracy or enjoying a pleasant melody. Originally, listening to the Quran with reflection while simultaneously enjoying its recitation weren’t separate experiences—they were one cohesive encounter with divine speech. That’s what true recitation is meant to be.

The ayah concludes with a direct address to these false worshippers: فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ—“So taste the punishment on account of the disbelief you engaged in.” Their mockery of true worship has earned them this consequence.

Ayah 36

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ لِيَصُدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَسَيُنفِقُونَهَا ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ

This ayah reveals the financial dimension of the disbelievers’ campaign against Islam. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ—“Those who disbelieve are spending their wealth”—and for what purpose? لِيَصُدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ—“to obstruct from the path of Allah ﷻ.”

Mobilizing an army of a thousand men with 900 camels and 100 horses, equipped with weapons and supplies—this required an enormous budget. The Quraysh invested heavily in their campaign to destroy the Muslim community.

But Allah ﷻ delivers a powerful prophecy in stages. First: فَسَيُنفِقُونَهَا—“They will spend it.” Don’t think they’ve exhausted their resources. They’ll spend even more. This is Allah’s promise—He will make them continue to pour wealth into their doomed campaign.

Second: ثُمَّ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسْرَةً—“Then it will be nothing but a source of regret for them.” After all that spending, after all that investment, they’ll look back and say, “How much money did we waste on this? How much wealth did we destroy in this war?”

Third: ثُمَّ يُغْلَبُونَ—“Then they will be overcome.” Despite all their spending, despite all their resources, they will be defeated.

The ayah concludes with their ultimate destination: وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ يُحْشَرُونَ—“And those who disbelieved—it is to Hellfire that they will be gathered.” Their spending in this world to obstruct Allah’s path leads to eternal loss in the Hereafter.

Ayah 37

لِيَمِيزَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُۥ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُۥ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُۥ فِى جَهَنَّمَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

This ayah explains the divine wisdom behind allowing this conflict: لِيَمِيزَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ—“So that Allah may distinguish the filthy from the good and pure.” The word يَمِيزَ means to separate, to distinguish clearly between two things.

What does this separation mean practically? First, it distinguishes the illegitimate custodians of the Kaaba from its rightful guardians. The Quraysh claimed authority over the Sacred House, but through this conflict, Allah ﷻ will establish who the true protectors are.

Second, this process exposes the hypocrites within the Muslim ranks. The battlefield becomes the mechanism through which hypocrisy is removed from the community. At the time of the Prophet ﷺ, battle served as a filter—those whose commitment was merely superficial would be revealed under pressure.

The ayah continues: وَيَجْعَلَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُۥ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—“And He may take the filthy and place some of it upon the other.” What does this mean? It refers to the alliance of evil—how the hypocrites were secretly sending messages to the enemy: “What are you waiting for? Just attack. We’ll back you up from the inside.”

This collaboration between external enemies and internal hypocrites would later be exposed, particularly with certain Jewish tribes who had treaties with the Muslims. On the surface, they maintained diplomatic relations, but secretly they were encouraging attacks, promising support from within. The phrase “pile it up one on top of the other” captures this layering of treachery upon treachery.

فَيَرْكُمَهُۥ جَمِيعًا—“So that He may pile it up altogether.” Allah ﷻ will gather all this filth together. فَيَجْعَلَهُۥ فِى جَهَنَّمَ—“Then He will place it in Hellfire.” The ultimate destination for all this accumulated evil.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ—“Those are the ones who are truly the ultimate losers.” Despite all their scheming, plotting, spending and collaboration, they end up with total loss.

Ayah 38

قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَنتَهُوا۟ يُغْفَرْ لَهُم مَّا قَدْ سَلَفَ وَإِن يَعُودُوا۟ فَقَدْ مَضَتْ سُنَّتُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

After describing the consequences of continued opposition, Allah ﷻ now opens a door of mercy. The Prophet ﷺ is commanded: قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟—“Say to those who disbelieve.”

The message is clear and generous: إِن يَنتَهُوا۟—“If they were to stop now”—stop the aggression, stop the obstruction, stop the warfare—يُغْفَرْ لَهُم مَّا قَدْ سَلَفَ—“they will be forgiven for whatever has already passed.” Forgiveness would be offered to them. No charges will be brought against them for their previous actions. Their past would be wiped clean.

This is remarkable mercy. After everything they’ve done—the persecution, the mockery, the warfare—if they simply stop now, Allah ﷻ offers complete forgiveness for all of it.

But the ayah presents the alternative: وَإِن يَعُودُوا۟—“And if they return” to the battlefield, if they come back with renewed aggression—فَقَدْ مَضَتْ سُنَّتُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ—“then the precedent of the earlier ones has already passed.”

In other words, the Quran has already been revealed. The descriptions of what happened to previous nations who continued to oppose their messengers after clear warnings—those accounts are already available. Allah ﷻ doesn’t need to repeat Himself. The pattern is established. The warning is clear. They already know what happened to nations that returned to aggression after the warning was given. Look at the legacy of those who came before and understand what awaits if you choose that path.

Ayah 39

وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ كُلُّهُۥ لِلَّهِ ۚ فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

This is post-Badr commentary. The battle has just concluded, but Allah ﷻ tells the Muslims: وَقَـٰتِلُوهُمْ—“And fight them.” The instruction is clear: the fight isn’t over, brothers. One battle has ended, but the larger struggle continues.

The objective is stated: حَتَّىٰ لَا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ—“Until there is no more fitnah.” The word فِتْنَةٌ here carries multiple meanings: trial, corruption, persecution, and the danger of attack. It encompasses all forms of oppression and threat against the Muslim community.

The ultimate goal: وَيَكُونَ ٱلدِّينُ كُلُّهُۥ لِلَّهِ—“And so that the religion, all of it, belongs to Allah ﷻ.” Fight until the religion can be practiced freely, until Allah’s message can be conveyed without obstruction, until worship is directed to Allah ﷻ alone without interference or persecution. At this point, the disbelievers still control the Qiblah, the spiritual center of the Muslim community. The mission isn’t complete.

But even here, mercy is offered: فَإِنِ ٱنتَهَوْا۟—“Then if they stop.” If they cease their aggression and obstruction.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ—“Then indeed, Allah is All-Seeing of whatever they do.” Allah ﷻ will inform you if they truly mean their cessation or not, because He sees inside their hearts. He’s the One who told you about the disease in their hearts. He’s the One who warned you about their plots. He’s the One who will tell you whether their stopping is genuine or just a temporary tactical retreat. Allah ﷻ knows what’s truly going on.

Ayah 40

وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ

The alternative scenario is now addressed: وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟—“And if they turn away,” if they turn back to fight you again, if they return to their aggression.

فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ—“Then know that Allah is your Mawla.” The word مَوْلَىٰ means your Protector, your Guardian, your Master who is actively engaged in protecting you. This isn’t just an expression of willingness to protect—it’s a declaration that He is currently, actively protecting you. He has just demonstrated this protection at Badr, and He continues to do so.

The ayah concludes with two powerful declarations:

نِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ—“What an excellent Mawla He is!” What an incredible Protector! This isn’t distant or theoretical protection—this is the One who just orchestrated your impossible victory against overwhelming odds.

وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ—“And what an excellent constant Helper!” The word نَصِيرُ emphasizes consistent, ongoing aid. He hasn’t just helped once; He is your constant source of support and assistance.

This ayah provides the believers with unshakeable confidence. Whatever comes next, whatever battles lie ahead, whoever turns against them—they have Allah ﷻ as their Mawla and Nasir. What more could they possibly need?

Ayah 41

۞ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا غَنِمْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُۥ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْفُرْقَانِ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Now we return to the subject that opened this Surah: the dispute about the spoils of war. Remember the controversy, the disagreements about who deserved what from the battlefield gains? Allah ﷻ now provides the definitive ruling.

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟—“You had better know,” this is not optional information—أَنَّمَا غَنِمْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ—“whatever you gain as ghanimah from anything.” The word غَنِمْتُم is significant. In Arabic, غَنِيمَة refers to wealth acquired without effort—something you simply picked up from the ground, found, and pocketed. Whatever you picked up effortlessly from the battlefield.

فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُۥ—“Then to Allah alone belongs one-fifth of it.” Twenty percent goes to Allah ﷻ first and foremost.

But practically, you don’t hand money directly to Allah ﷻ. So how is this fifth distributed? The ayah explains:

وَلِلرَّسُولِ—“And to the Messenger”—notice the لِـ (lam) connecting this directly to Allah’s share. The Messenger ﷺ, as head of state, will have a salary from this treasury. He has expenses, responsibilities.

وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ—“And to the close relatives of the Prophet ﷺ.” He has the right to directly spend on his family from this allocation. Again, the لِـ connects this group, showing they’re part of the same direct allocation tied to the Prophet’s role and household.

Then the لِـ separates for the next categories: وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ—“And the orphans,” وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ—“and those who cannot help themselves,” وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ—“and the traveler in need.”

Why is the family of the Prophet ﷺ mentioned separately with direct connection to his allocation? Because Allah ﷻ is preempting controversy. If the Prophet ﷺ spends on his family from general treasury funds, critics could say, “Look at him spending on his relatives. We went to battle and his family gets extra provisions, extra blankets.” So Allah ﷻ makes it explicit: it’s not a controversy because I’m authorizing this. He will spend on his family from this budget, and this is My decree.

Of course, he’s not taking the entire 20% for himself and his family. The treasury is for public welfare generally, with particular attention to the vulnerable: orphans, those who can’t help themselves, and travelers in difficulty.

Then comes the powerful closing: إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ—“If you truly have iman in Allah ﷻ.” This phrase إِن كُنتُمْ isn’t presenting an option—it’s making this a matter of faith. If you claim to believe, then this ruling is not up for discussion or negotiation. This is what your iman requires.

وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْفُرْقَانِ—“And in what We sent down upon Our slave on the Day of Furqan.” The Day of Criterion, the day when truth and falsehood became absolutely clear to both sides. Even the disbelievers understood that day whose side Allah ﷻ was on—they had asked for His verdict through battle, and they received it.

يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ—“The day on which the two large gatherings clashed with one another.” The two armies, the two massive groups coming together in battle.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—“And Allah has complete control over all things.” This is His verdict on the matter of spoils. He who gave you the impossible victory, who orchestrated every detail of that day, who has power over everything—He has now given you His ruling. Just take it. Accept it. This isn’t a matter for debate.

Ayah 42

إِذْ أَنتُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلْقُصْوَىٰ وَٱلرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ تَوَاعَدتُّمْ لَٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْمِيعَـٰدِ ۙ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَىَّ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah takes us back to the scene of Badr, painting the geographical reality of that day. The word عُدْوَة refers to elevated ground on either end of a valley. Picture a large field with elevated positions at both ends—like mini-hills on either side of a valley.

إِذْ أَنتُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا—“When you were at the closer elevated ground.” The Muslims were positioned on the near end.

وَهُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلْقُصْوَىٰ—“And they were at the far elevated ground.” The Quraysh army was positioned on the distant end, elevated on their side.

وَٱلرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ—“And the caravan was below you.” The trade caravan that started this whole affair—Abu Sufyan’s caravan with all its valuable goods—had passed by the coast, below the Muslim position.

Now comes the remarkable observation: وَلَوْ تَوَاعَدتُّمْ لَٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْمِيعَـٰدِ—“And had you two armies made an appointment to meet, you would have violated the promise.” If the Muslim army and the Quraysh army had tried to coordinate and set a meeting time for battle—“Let’s meet at Badr, three o’clock in the afternoon”—you would have shown up at different times. Someone would have been early, someone late. The appointment would have fallen apart.

But that’s not what happened. Despite no coordination, despite no communication between the armies, both sides showed up at exactly the same time, on opposite ends of the battlefield, perfectly positioned to see each other. The precision was impossible by human planning.

وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا—“However, this occurred so that Allah might accomplish a matter that was already decreed.” The phrase كَانَ مَفْعُولًا is fascinating—it means “that which had to be,” a done deal. In English, we might say “a done deal is done.” That’s essentially what مَفْعُولًا means—finished, completed, settled, no changes coming.

Why did Allah ﷻ orchestrate this precise meeting? لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ—“So that whoever would die a violent death would die upon clear proof.” The word هَلَكَ means to die violently, which is what happens in battle. The point is that even those who died on the side of disbelief would die having seen clear evidence of whose side Allah ﷻ was on.

وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَىَّ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ—“And whoever would live would live upon clear proof.” Those who survived the battle, whether from the Muslim side or the enemy side, would also live with absolute clarity about the truth.

Some scholars have understood this in a deeper spiritual sense as well: whoever “dies” (meaning the martyrs) didn’t truly die—بَلْ أَحْيَاءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ—“Rather, they are alive with their Lord” (3:169). So they’re living upon that clear proof. And whoever survives among the Muslims also lives with the clear proof that has been sent to them by Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a reminder of Allah’s comprehensive awareness: وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ—“And certainly Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.” He heard every word spoken that day—every challenge, every prayer, every cry. And He knows everything—every intention, every fear, every hope. Nothing escaped His hearing, nothing escaped His knowledge. The One who orchestrated this entire encounter with perfect precision is the One who hears and knows all.

Ayah 43–53 · linked video: 07. Al-’Anfal (Ayah 43-53)

Surah Al-Anfal: Ayahs 43-53

Ayah 43

إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى مَنَامِكَ قَلِيلًا ۖ وَلَوْ أَرَىٰكَهُمْ كَثِيرًا لَّفَشِلْتُمْ وَلَتَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَلَّمَ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

Allah ﷻ speaks directly to His Messenger ﷺ about a vision given before the Battle of Badr: “When He showed you, their army in your dream as few.” This divine vision showed the enemy forces as قَلِيلًا - very little in number.

Some critics have misunderstood this ayah, claiming it represents a false dream since the Muslims were actually outnumbered. However, this misses the deeper meaning. Allah ﷻ wasn’t deceiving His Prophet ﷺ - He was showing him the true quality and worth of that army, not merely their numbers. The power of any army comes from its motivation, discipline and unity. You can have massive numbers that scatter and disperse when tested. Allah ﷻ revealed to the Prophet ﷺ the actual reality (حَقِيقَة) of their forces - they were genuinely weak despite their size.

The ayah continues: وَلَوْ أَرَىٰكَهُمْ كَثِيرًا لَّفَشِلْتُمْ - “And had He shown them to you as many, you would have lost heart.” The word فَشِلْتُمْ means to lose motivation and courage. This isn’t a mere possibility - Allah ﷻ states it as a certainty because He knows exactly what would have happened.

وَلَتَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ - “And you would have disputed in the matter.” The believers would have pulled at each other in disagreement. Some would have argued they should retreat to the coast, others that they should have stayed home. The unity would have fractured through internal conflict.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَلَّمَ - “But Allah gave safety.” He instilled peace and calm in everyone’s hearts. The word سَلَّمَ carries the meaning of making something safe and secure, protecting it from harm.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ - “Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts.” Allah ﷻ knows what lies inside our hearts and chests. When He says “you would have argued,” it’s not speculation - it’s certain knowledge of human nature and what would have absolutely occurred.

Ayah 44

وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ ٱلْتَقَيْتُمْ فِىٓ أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِىٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ لِيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

This ayah shifts from the dream vision to the actual moment of confrontation. وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ ٱلْتَقَيْتُمْ - “And when He showed them to you, when you met.” Both armies were positioned on elevated ground with clear vantage points, able to see each other plainly.

فِىٓ أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا - “In your eyes as few.” Allah ﷻ made the Muslims perceive the enemy as smaller than they actually were. This gave them confidence and discipline.

وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِىٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ - “And He made you appear as few in their eyes.” Notice the different verb structure. Allah ﷻ doesn’t simply say “He showed them to you as few” the same way. Instead, He uses يُقَلِّلُكُمْ - He diminished you. The Quraysh didn’t just see fewer numbers - they saw weakness. They looked at the Muslim ranks and thought: “I don’t see any swords with them. That one looks like a farmer. These people can’t even fight properly.”

This perception made the enemy overconfident and relaxed. They weren’t mentally prepared for what was coming. Meanwhile, the Muslims remained disciplined and encouraged without being overwhelmed by the sight of superior numbers.

لِيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا - “So that Allah might accomplish a matter already destined.” Both sides seeing each other as less was part of Allah’s divine plan to execute a decree that had to pass.

وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ - “And to Allah are all matters returned.” The outcome of battle, victory and loss - these aren’t in human hands. The final decision returns to Allah ﷻ alone.

Ayah 45

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَٱثْبُتُوا۟ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the believers with direct commands: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً - “O you who have believed, when you encounter a group.” When you meet any group in battle, any army.

فَٱثْبُتُوا۟ - “Then stand firm.” Keep your feet planted. This is remarkable because it’s guidance for what will happen again. In the Battle of Uhud, three years after Badr, some Muslims would abandon their positions despite clear orders. Allah ﷻ is already warning them of what’s to come: when it happens again, maintain your positions. To Him, the matter is already decided - He’s not predicting what might happen, but addressing what will happen.

وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا - “And remember Allah much.” In the heat of battle, in the midst of fear and chaos, keep remembering Allah ﷻ abundantly. This is the spiritual anchor that keeps the believer grounded.

لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ - “That you may be successful.” Success comes through firmness and remembrance, not through superior numbers or weaponry.

Ayah 46

وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَا تَنَـٰزَعُوا۟ فَتَفْشَلُوا۟ وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ ۖ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ - “And obey Allah and His Messenger.” This establishes the foundation of success.

وَلَا تَنَـٰزَعُوا۟ - “And do not dispute.” Notice Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “don’t argue with Allah and His Messenger” - He says don’t argue with each other. This is precisely what happened at Uhud when the Muslims disputed among themselves about strategy. Some wanted to fight from inside Madinah, others wanted to go out and meet the enemy. These internal conflicts preceded the difficulties they faced.

فَتَفْشَلُوا۟ - “Lest you lose courage.” The word فَشِلْتُمْ in modern Arabic means simply “to fail,” but in classical Arabic it carries the deeper meaning of losing motivation entirely. Imagine volunteering to help at an Islamic organization, attending your first meeting, and witnessing nothing but arguments and people talking over each other. Two hours pass of this chaos. How motivated are you afterwards? The wind has been taken out of your sails.

When people pull at each other, especially in religious matters, motivation evaporates. The whole purpose of gathering should be to increase determination and fire each other up for good work - not to drain each other’s energy.

وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ - “And your strength would depart.” The word رِيح means wind or breath. The image is of someone running hard, panting heavily, running out of breath. Or picture a ship with its sails up, moving forward on the wind - then suddenly the wind stops and it sits dead in the water, motionless. That’s what internal conflict does to a community’s forward momentum.

وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ - “And be patient.” Remain constant and persevering through difficulties.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ - “Indeed, Allah is with the patient.” His special company and support is with those who maintain sabr.

This ayah teaches us something critical about preserving motivation in Islamic work. The ayah begins with obeying Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, but then immediately addresses what destroys motivation most: internal arguments and disputes. When you’re part of any positive effort and arguing breaks out, keep the work going but distance yourself from the arguments. Don’t get entangled. These conflicts will destroy whatever small flame of motivation you’re carrying. That motivation is like a weak candle burning on a windy day - it could be extinguished at any moment. Protect it.

Shaytan is expert at making personalities clash in Islamic work. People take things personally when their opinions aren’t adopted. But here’s the distinction: you should be concerned when Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ are being disobeyed. That’s worth being upset about. As for disputes about which textbook to use, whether to meet Monday or Tuesday, which approach to take - these aren’t worth fracturing over. Let those things slide. They’re not the end of the world. If they bother you to the point of conflict, you’ll end up pulling at each other and the entire organizational effort will collapse.

Ayah 47

وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بَطَرًا وَرِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ

Allah ﷻ now contrasts the believers with the army of Quraysh: وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بَطَرًا - “And do not be like those who came out from their homes insolently.”

The word بَطَرًا is one of several terms for arrogance in Arabic. It specifically means showing off based on wealth and status, and mistreating others because of your position. It’s the elite person abusing the cab driver, the wealthy family being rude to the restaurant waiter, the boss degrading his secretary - all because of perceived superiority in wealth or power. The Quraysh marched out with this attitude: “Who can even look us in the eye? Nobody can match us.”

وَرِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ - “And to be seen by people.” They wanted to make a show, to display their power and dominance.

وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - “And to obstruct from the path of Allah.” Despite all their posturing and self-justification, Allah ﷻ reduces their motivations to this: they were blocking people from Allah’s path - both themselves and others.

وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ - “And Allah is encompassing of what they do.” Everything they’re planning, every move they make, is completely surrounded and enclosed by Allah’s knowledge and power. They cannot escape His awareness.

Ayah 48

وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَقَالَ لَا غَالِبَ لَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَإِنِّى جَارٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَرَآءَتِ ٱلْفِئَتَانِ نَكَصَ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ وَقَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ مَا لَا تَرَوْنَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ - “And when Shaytan made their deeds pleasing to them.” This requires careful understanding. The word أَعْمَـٰل generally refers to good deeds in Arabic. When it means evil deeds, it’s usually qualified with an adjective. So what does it mean that Shaytan beautified their “deeds” for them?

He made them see their actions as righteous. They were custodians of the Kaaba - surely that’s noble work. When they committed crimes against Muslims, Shaytan whispered: “You’re protecting your nation, your tribe, your heritage and future. This is good.” Nobody thinks of themselves as a wrongdoer, especially in battle. Both sides see themselves as heroes with noble causes. Shaytan’s work is to make them believe deeply in their cause as the ultimate good while viewing the other side as ultimate evil.

وَقَالَ لَا غَالِبَ لَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ - “And he said: ‘No one can overcome you today from among the people.’”

وَإِنِّى جَارٌ لَّكُمْ - “And indeed, I am your protector.” There’s linguistic subtlety here. Shaytan says جَارٌ لَّكُمْ - “a neighbor/protector to you” - which doesn’t indicate permanence. If he wanted to make it permanent, he would have said إِنِّى جَارُكُمْ - “I am your protector.” The grammatical structure he uses allows for temporary association. We’ll see why in a moment.

فَلَمَّا تَرَآءَتِ ٱلْفِئَتَانِ - “But when the two armies sighted each other.” Both groups saw one another clearly.

نَكَصَ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ - “He turned back on his heels.” The verb نَكَصَ is fascinating. It specifically means to turn away from doing something good. First Shaytan convinced them they were engaged in noble work - protecting their tribe, preserving their honor. Then he turned away from this “good deed,” abandoning it entirely. When he saw the Muslims, he didn’t just see them - he saw the army of angels. These creatures of light. When he witnessed that, he was done.

وَقَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكُمْ - “And said: ‘Indeed, I am disassociated from you.’” Notice he doesn’t address them directly - he’s speaking to himself. Allah ﷻ captures Shaytan’s internal dialogue.

إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ مَا لَا تَرَوْنَ - “Indeed, I see what you do not see.” If Shaytan had whispered this to the Quraysh, they would have realized there was a problem. But he doesn’t care to warn them. He’s simply talking to himself, and Allah ﷻ reveals it to us.

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ - “Indeed, I fear Allah.” Even Shaytan fears Allah ﷻ before the Day of Judgment.

وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - “And Allah is severe in penalty.”

What we learn here is powerful: Shaytan got scared and ran from the battlefield before Judgment Day even arrived. If he fled out of fear of Allah ﷻ, what does that tell us? In the next ayah, we’ll see hypocrites speaking, and you might think Shaytan must be whispering to them. But Allah ﷻ has already shown us he’s gone and he’s afraid. The hypocrites are doing this entirely on their own. This reveals something critical about human responsibility - we can’t always blame Shaytan. Sometimes our own nafs, our own ego and desires, lead us astray.

Ayah 49

إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ غَرَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ دِينُهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ - “When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease said.” These are the people who stayed behind, who didn’t join the Muslim army.

غَرَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ دِينُهُمْ - “Their religion has deluded them.” They looked at the believers who followed the Messenger ﷺ so completely and thought them foolish. “Why are they so deceived? Why has religion blinded them so much? Why can’t they see they’re destroying themselves? Their extremism has made them stupid.”

The hypocrites saw themselves as the clear-sighted ones and called the believers blind. They viewed complete trust in Allah ﷻ and following His Messenger ﷺ as irrational fanaticism. To an outsider without iman, that step toward the battlefield against overwhelming odds appears insane. They don’t have تَوَكُّل - they don’t have trust and reliance on Allah ﷻ. Without that trust, the believers’ actions seem crazy.

وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ - “But whoever relies upon Allah.” This is the believers’ “problem” from the hypocrites’ perspective. The entire motivation to march out with that small army came from the Messenger’s ﷺ guarantee of victory through Allah’s help. The only thing driving this campaign is tawakkul - complete reliance on Allah ﷻ. Without that trust, there’s no reason to take even one step in that direction.

But whoever has trusted in Allah ﷻ should also know something:

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ - “Then indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.” Allah ﷻ is عَزِيز - the Ultimate Authority who has the power to destroy that massive army. And Allah ﷻ doesn’t call you to do anything foolish or reckless. He is حَكِيم - full of wisdom. This decision the believers made, relying on Allah’s authority and trusting in His wisdom, is the only rational choice for one who truly believes.

Ayah 50

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۙ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۙ - “And if you could but see when the angels take the souls of those who disbelieved.” The expression لَوْ تَرَىٰ - “if you could only see” - draws attention to something we cannot witness but Allah ﷻ describes for us.

ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ - “The angels striking their faces and their backs.” These disbelievers were killed by Muslims on the battlefield - that’s the first beating. Then when the angels come to take their souls, they receive a second beating. They’re struck on their faces and their backs. When someone gets hit in the face, the natural response is to cover the face - which exposes the back. The angels strike the back, so they grab their back - which exposes the face again. This continues back and forth.

وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ - “And taste the punishment of the Burning Fire.” As the angels strike them, they’re told to taste the torment of the flames that await them.

Ayah 51

ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ

ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ - “That is for what your hands have put forth.” This punishment is directly because of the investments you made with your own hands, what you sent ahead for yourselves.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ - “And because Allah is not ever unjust to His servants.” Allah ﷻ will never wrong any of His slaves. This punishment is earned, deserved and just.

Ayah 52

كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - “Like the custom of the people of Fir’awn (Pharaoh).” The word دَأْب means continuous practice, a habitual way of doing things, a legacy that continues. It can refer to a dynasty that lasts a long time - and the longer a dynasty stays in power, the more stable and established it becomes.

وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ - “And those before them.” This references nations that came even before Fir’awn’s dynasty, extending the pattern back through history.

كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - “They disbelieved in the signs of Allah.” Despite all the signs and evidences, they rejected Allah’s ayat.

فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ - “So Allah seized them for their sins.” This is the inevitable consequence of persistent rejection and continuous sin.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - “Indeed, Allah is Powerful and severe in penalty.” He is قَوِىّ - All-Powerful, and شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - intense and severe in executing punishment.

Ayah 53

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

This profound ayah establishes a divine principle that works in two directions.

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ - “That is because Allah would not change a favor which He had bestowed upon a people.” Allah ﷻ doesn’t take away blessings He has given to a nation arbitrarily.

حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ - “Until they change what is in themselves.” The change must come from within them first.

We’ve seen this principle stated earlier in the opposite direction: Allah ﷻ does not improve the condition of a people until they change what is within themselves. Here we see the other side: Allah ﷻ does not remove His blessings and favors until a people change themselves for the worse.

Consider the nations of the past. Fir’awn had massive armies. Where did that power come from? Allah ﷻ gave it. The people of Saba’ had incredible business practices and trade routes. The people of Thamud could carve homes out of mountains - who gave them that physical strength and ability? Allah ﷻ blessed them with these capabilities.

Allah ﷻ would never take those blessings away until they themselves changed what was in their hearts - until they corrupted themselves from within. The external collapse follows the internal corruption. Nations don’t fall because Allah ﷻ is unjust - they fall because they changed themselves first.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - “And indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.” He is سَمِيع - All-Hearing of what people say and how they speak about His blessings. And He is عَلِيم - All-Knowing of what changes occur in their hearts, what transformations take place in their inner selves.

This ayah serves as both warning and hope: Warning that blessings can be lost through internal corruption, and hope that change for the better begins from within and can bring about Allah’s transformation of our circumstances.

Ayah 54–63 · linked video: 08. Al-’Anfal (Ayah 54-63)

Surah Al-Anfal: Ayahs 54-63

Ayah 54

كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ وَكُلٌّ كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ

This ayah establishes a historical pattern of divine justice. The phrase كَدَأْبِ means “like the continual legacy” or “like the established pattern.” Allah ﷻ is drawing a parallel between the disbelievers of Makkah and the nations that came before them, particularly the people of Pharaoh.

The ayah says: Like the continual legacy of the people of Pharaoh, and those who came from much before them—they lied against the miraculous signs of their Master. The word كَذَّبُوا۟ emphasizes that they didn’t merely reject the signs; they actively lied against them, denying their divine origin despite witnessing their miraculous nature.

The consequence follows immediately: فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ—We violently destroyed them because of their sins. The verb أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم carries the meaning of complete, overwhelming destruction. Then Allah ﷻ mentions specifically: وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ—and We drowned the lineage of Pharaoh, the entire family.

The ayah concludes with a comprehensive judgment: وَكُلٌّ كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ—and all of them were wrongdoers. This establishes that their punishment wasn’t arbitrary but earned through their persistent injustice and rejection of truth.

Ayah 55

إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

This is one of the most striking ayahs in terms of how Allah ﷻ describes those who persist in disbelief. The word ٱلدَّوَآبِّ means “beasts” or creatures that walk on the earth. Allah ﷻ doesn’t even dignify persistent disbelievers with the status of humanity in this context.

From a philosophical perspective, this requires understanding what makes humans superior to other creatures. When Allah ﷻ created Adam (AS), He blew into him from His spirit (الرُّوح). The Quran says: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى (When I have fashioned him and blown into him from My spirit) (15:29). This spirit is attributed to Allah ﷻ Himself—something so honored that He commanded the angels to prostrate in acknowledgment of this elevated creation.

The spirit, this divine breath, is what truly makes us human. In a hadith, the Prophet ﷺ said that the first thing Allah ﷻ created was the pen, but in describing the spirit, he referred to it as light—نُور. This powerful creation of Allah ﷻ is what distinguishes us from animals.

When a disbeliever denies the truth, he essentially buries this spirit within himself. He suppresses the fitrah (natural disposition) that recognizes the Creator. After that denial becomes entrenched, after the spiritual element dies within, what remains? Only the body. And a being that seeks only to feed its body, shelter it and satisfy its physical needs—what distinguishes that from an animal? An animal has no higher aspirations beyond its physical existence.

This is why Allah ﷻ says these disbelievers are إِنَّ شَرَّ ٱلدَّوَآبِّ (the worst of beasts). They were given the highest status among creation, and then they reduced themselves to something lower than the animals. The phrase عِندَ ٱللَّهِ (in Allah’s sight) emphasizes that this is His judgment, His assessment of their worth.

The ayah concludes: ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (those who disbelieve, for they will not believe). This describes a state of permanent closure to faith, a heart that has sealed itself against guidance.

Ayah 56

ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتَّ مِنْهُمْ ثُمَّ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِى كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يَتَّقُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ becomes more specific, referring to certain Jewish tribes in Medina. The historical context is important here: these tribes had sent secret communications to Makkah, promising that if the Quraysh attacked, they would help from the inside.

The word عَـٰهَدتَّ is significant—it means “you made a mutual covenant with them.” The covenant was bilateral, not one-sided. The verb comes from the root ع-ه-د, which implies a sacred promise or treaty. The phrase ٱلَّذِينَ عَـٰهَدتَّ مِنْهُمْ (those from whom you took a treaty) indicates specific people, not all Jews, but particular groups who entered into agreements.

Then comes the violation: ثُمَّ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَهُمْ فِى كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ (then they break their covenant every single time). The word يَنقُضُونَ means to violate, break or tear apart. The phrase فِى كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ emphasizes the pattern—every opportunity they get, every occasion, they violate their promises.

The ayah ends with a damning assessment: وَهُمْ لَا يَتَّقُونَ (and they have no taqwa). They don’t fear the consequences. They lack that consciousness of Allah ﷻ that would restrain them from treachery.

This ayah establishes a crucial ethical principle that addresses modern allegations about taqiyya (deception) in Islam. Some claim that Muslims will sign treaties and seek protection in a state while covertly planning to attack. These ayahs give us the ethics of enmity and the rules of engagement.

Allah ﷻ identifies the worst type of creature as one who makes a promise and violates it, who makes a treaty with somebody and breaks that treaty. This is the foundation: if a Muslim makes any treaty—a treaty of citizenship, an agreement to abide by the laws of a country, any covenant—he is bound by it absolutely. The only way he can violate that treaty is to openly declare that he no longer abides by it, to publicly announce his withdrawal from the agreement and his declaration of enmity. If he doesn’t do that, he has no right to violate any single instruction within that treaty.

This is Islamic ethics at its clearest. There is no room for covert operations against those with whom you have a treaty.

Ayah 57

فَإِمَّا تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَرْبِ فَشَرِّدْ بِهِم مَّنْ خَلْفَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ

This ayah deals with what happens when these treaty violators are caught on the battlefield. The word تَثْقَفَنَّهُمْ means to catch them, to find them, to encounter them in battle.

Now, when they’re caught, you might go back to their Jewish tribe and say, “We had an agreement. What happened?” They would respond, “He doesn’t represent us. He’s a rogue element. We didn’t authorize him.” Even if they did authorize the action, they would disavow all knowledge—a covert operation with plausible deniability. Like agents who get caught and claim, “I was just a tourist. I was doing this on my own.” They’re trained to deny their connection to any governing authority.

So what should be done with people like that? You can’t prove the connection definitively, and they might even be telling the truth about the person acting independently. Allah ﷻ gives the instruction: فَشَرِّدْ بِهِم مَّنْ خَلْفَهُمْ (give them such punishment that those behind them are terrified).

The word فَشَرِّدْ comes from a root that means to scatter or disperse in fear. In Arabic usage, شَرَدَ is used when you slaughter one camel and all the other camels run away in terror. It’s a powerful word indicating not just fear but panic-inducing terror.

The punishment should be such that whoever is puppeteering behind the scenes, whoever is sitting in Medina coordinating these violations, would jump out of their seat in terror. They would be so frightened that they would think twice before sending anyone else. لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (so that they may take heed, so they remember). The punishment serves as a deterrent, a reminder of the consequences of treaty violation.

This is about individual violators. The punishment is individual, but it carries a message to the collective. If there was a secret covert connection, this ensures they don’t send another operative because they know what happens.

Ayah 58

وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً فَٱنۢبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ

The previous ayah dealt with individuals who violated treaties. This ayah addresses what to do when you fear an entire nation is acting treacherously.

The phrase وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً (and if you fear treachery from a people) indicates not just a suspicion but a growing confidence that a whole nation is shaking your hand to your face while planning against you behind your back.

What should you do? فَٱنۢبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ (throw it back to them on equal terms). The word ٱنۢبِذْ means to throw back, to cast away. The phrase عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ means “on equal footing” or “openly and evenly.” In other words: go back to them and say openly, “We are no longer in agreement.”

They might protest, “No, no, we’re not planning anything!” You respond, “We want to be on the safe side. We don’t trust you anymore. We think you’re planning against us.” The Islamic approach is not to continue acting like you’re friends while considering them enemies secretly. Instead, you openly declare, “I no longer want to act as your friend. I’m calling you an enemy openly. Let’s just be clear about where we stand.”

This establishes the foreign policy of Islam: you don’t act against a nation with whom you have a treaty. You don’t send spies to a nation with which you have peace relations. If you have an agreement, if you have peaceful diplomatic relations with a nation, then you cannot act against them in any covert way.

In modern politics, even nations that are friends with each other send spies to one another. This is considered normal, “just how the world works.” There have been cases of intelligence sharing violations even between the closest allies. But Muslims don’t operate on this platform. If Muslims have peaceful relations with a nation, they cannot have covert operations against that nation behind their backs.

If they must do something against that nation, they first have to declare: “All of our diplomacy is dead. We’re letting you know upfront.” فَٱنۢبِذْ إِلَيْهِمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ—then deal with them face to face, on equal terms, with full transparency.

This is the ethics of Muslim foreign policy. It’s a powerful ethical principle that would have put Muslims at a disadvantage in gathering intelligence. They could have gained much more information from Makkah and Medina by keeping things covert. But Allah ﷻ doesn’t permit this kind of behavior. He expects a higher standard from Muslims.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ (Indeed, Allah does not love the deceitful). The word ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ means those who betray trust, those who act treacherously. This is a categorical statement: deception in treaties is something Allah ﷻ does not love.

Yes, war involves tactics and strategy. Once war is declared, then you use your military tactics. But when war hasn’t been declared, when you’re in a treaty relationship, you have no right to act covertly against that party.

Ayah 59

وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَبَقُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَا يُعْجِزُونَ

After the Battle of Badr, the Quraysh retreated to Makkah in defeat. Seventy of their great leaders had been killed, many were taken captive, and there was tremendous humiliation. A war cry began to build in Makkah: “We must take revenge.”

Allah ﷻ tells the believers: وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَبَقُوٓا۟ (Don’t let those who disbelieve think for a second that they have gotten ahead, that they’ve escaped). The word سَبَقُوٓا means to get ahead, to escape, to outpace someone.

The message is clear: don’t be intimidated by the fact that they’re going to come back strong. إِنَّهُمْ لَا يُعْجِزُونَ (They will not be able to overpower you). The word يُعْجِزُونَ comes from the root meaning to make someone unable, to overpower, to render someone incapable.

This ayah is psychological preparation. Yes, there was a victory at Badr, but this is just the beginning. The enemy will regroup, they’ll plan their revenge, they’ll come back stronger. Don’t let that intimidate you or make you think they’ve gained the upper hand.

Ayah 60

وَأَعِدُّوا۟ لَهُم مَّا ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن قُوَّةٍ وَمِن رِّبَاطِ ٱلْخَيْلِ تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِۦ عَدُوَّ ٱللَّهِ وَعَدُوَّكُمْ وَءَاخَرِينَ مِن دُونِهِمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ

Since the believers know the enemy will be preparing, they must prepare as well. وَأَعِدُّوا۟ لَهُم (And prepare for them) مَّا ٱسْتَطَعْتُم مِّن قُوَّةٍ (whatever you can of power/strength). This is comprehensive—any kind of power you can assemble.

Then Allah ﷻ gives a specific example from their context: وَمِن رِّبَاطِ ٱلْخَيْلِ (and of tethered horses). The word رِّبَاطِ means horses that are tied, kept in stables, ready to be unhitched and deployed immediately. These are horses on standby, like military units on alert status, code red, ready to ship out the moment the call comes.

The purpose: تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِۦ عَدُوَّ ٱللَّهِ وَعَدُوَّكُمْ (You will terrify by means of it the enemy of Allah and your enemy). The word تُرْهِبُونَ means to strike fear, to create terror in the hearts of the enemy. This is psychological warfare through preparedness. When the enemy sends spies and sees the army is always ready to deploy, always in a state of readiness, they become terrified. They see you’re not sleeping on the job.

Then comes a fascinating addition: وَءَاخَرِينَ مِن دُونِهِمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ (and others besides them whom you do not know—Allah knows them).

Who are these others? At this point in Medina’s history, the clear enemies were the Quraysh of Makkah and any Jewish tribes that violated their treaties. But there was another group—the hypocrites (المنافقون). They appeared to be Muslims but secretly worked against the community.

Notice how Allah ﷻ phrases this: لَا تَعْلَمُونَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُهُمْ (You don’t know them; Allah knows them). By saying “Allah knows them,” He removed the license from Muslims to identify who is and isn’t a hypocrite. When Allah ﷻ says “He is the one who knows them,” it means you don’t get to say, “I think that person is a hypocrite” or “I suspect this one.” That judgment belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

The ayah concludes with a promise about spending: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ يُوَفَّ إِلَيْكُمْ (And whatever you spend in the path of Allah will be repaid to you in full) وَأَنتُمْ لَا تُظْلَمُونَ (and you will not be wronged).

It’s important to note the phrase فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (in the path of Allah). This specific phrase in the Quran is used when money is spent furthering the mission that was given to the Prophet ﷺ—making Islam victorious, establishing the deen.

This is different from general charity. When you spend on orphans, give to the poor, or provide someone a loan, that’s rewarded, but it’s not specifically called “fi sabilillah” in the Quranic usage. In these ayahs, fi sabilillah refers to military expeditions, preparing for hijrah, or other efforts directly supporting the prophetic mission.

Today we’ve generalized the term—building masajid, schools, community centers all fall under “fi sabilillah” in common usage. That’s not wrong, but understanding the Quran’s context helps us see that originally it meant: spending to further the specific mission the Messenger ﷺ was given.

Ayah 61

۞ وَإِن جَنَحُوا۟ لِلسَّلْمِ فَٱجْنَحْ لَهَا وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

The word جَنَحُوا literally means to lower the wing. Birds raise their wings when they’re about to attack—like a rooster or chicken spreading its wings in aggression. When a bird lowers its wings, it’s at peace, non-threatening.

وَإِن جَنَحُوا۟ لِلسَّلْمِ (If they incline toward peace)—if they lower their wings, put down their weapons—فَٱجْنَحْ لَهَا (then you incline toward it as well). Lower your weapons too. Accept the peace.

But then comes the crucial instruction: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ (And place your trust in Allah). Why does Allah ﷻ add this? Because you might think, “This is a trap. They’re not sincere. They’re just trying to buy time to regroup.”

Allah ﷻ is saying: at that point, don’t rely on your assessment of their intentions. Rely on Me. Trust Me. You don’t know their hearts, but I do. إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, All-Knowing).

I hear what their secret plans are. I know what they’re plotting. You just trust Me. If they come to you with intentions of peace, don’t worry about what they really mean. Let Me worry about that. You put your arms down, accept the peace, and trust in My wisdom and My protection.

This is a remarkable teaching about the ethics of peace. Even in a state of conflict, when the enemy extends an offer of peace, the Muslim response is to accept it and trust in Allah ﷻ, rather than to be perpetually suspicious and reject peace out of paranoia.

Ayah 62

وَإِن يُرِيدُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْدَعُوكَ فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِۦ وَبِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah addresses the concern raised by the previous one: what if they do intend to deceive?

وَإِن يُرِيدُوٓا۟ أَن يَخْدَعُوكَ (And if they intend to deceive you) فَإِنَّ حَسْبَكَ ٱللَّهُ (then indeed Allah is sufficient for you). The word حَسْبَكَ means “enough for you”—Allah ﷻ alone is sufficient, He is all you need.

But this isn’t the first time the Prophet ﷺ has had to rely completely on Allah ﷻ. The ayah reminds him: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَيَّدَكَ بِنَصْرِهِۦ (He is the One who aided you with His help). The word أَيَّدَكَ means to support, to strengthen, to aid.

Then comes something beautiful: وَبِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and with the believers). Allah ﷻ is speaking directly to the Messenger ﷺ, and He places the believers in the same category as His own divine help. This is an incredible honor bestowed upon the believers—they are mentioned as part of Allah’s aid to His Messenger ﷺ.

Allah ﷻ aided the Prophet ﷺ in two ways: بِنَصْرِهِۦ (with His help—meaning the angels and divine intervention) وَبِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and with the believers—meaning the community of faithful supporters).

This means that when we truly try to do the work that the Messenger ﷺ did, when we try to establish community the way he established it, we are aiding his mission. And by doing that, we are honored by being called part of Allah’s help, just as this ayah honors the early believers.

This wasn’t just about the believers during the Prophet’s lifetime. His mission is still alive, still calling for help. And we’re supposed to be the ones who answer that call, who say labbayk—“we’re here”—to that mission.

Ayah 63

وَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ ۚ لَوْ أَنفَقْتَ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مَّآ أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

When the Prophet ﷺ arrived in Medina, after establishing the masjid, what was the second thing he did? He established the brotherhood—the Mu’akhat—between the Muhajirun and the Ansar. This ayah speaks about that miraculous transformation.

وَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ (And He joined their hearts together). The word أَلَّفَ means to bring together, to create harmony, to join. But this wasn’t just surface-level cooperation—this was hearts being joined.

Then Allah ﷻ emphasizes the impossibility of this achievement through human means: لَوْ أَنفَقْتَ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مَّآ أَلَّفْتَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِهِمْ (If you had spent everything on the earth, all of it together, you would not have joined their hearts).

Imagine spending unlimited resources on a media campaign, public relations, cultural exchange programs—anything to get these people to stop hating each other. It would not have worked. Why? Because of tribal mentality.

The Arabs of that time were intensely tribal. Your tribe was your identity, your honor, your everything. The other tribe was the enemy by default. When tribalism becomes entrenched, when it’s set in its ways, it’s nearly impossible to erase. No amount of money, no social program, no political maneuvering can eliminate deeply rooted tribal hatred.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَلَّفَ بَيْنَهُمْ (But Allah joined between them). It was only through Allah’s intervention, through the power of His words in the Quran, through the transformative message of Islam, that these hearts came together.

We’re living in a world today where Muslims are still tribal—not just across the ocean but here, in America. Racism and tribalism persist within our communities in ways that shame us. There’s automatic prejudice based on ethnicity, nationality, even which village someone comes from.

The early Muslims were very tribal too, but Allah ﷻ erased that through the Quran. He got rid of that toxic identity politics through His revelation.

We have an opportunity in America that doesn’t exist anywhere else in the Muslim world. How many different nationalities stand together in one row for prayer? How many different ethnicities sit together in our gatherings? It’s remarkable. The only other place you see that kind of diversity is in the Haram during Hajj.

Yet despite standing together and reciting the word of Allah ﷻ together, are our hearts truly together? The Quran describes those whose appearance suggests unity but whose hearts are scattered: تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعًا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّىٰ (You would think they are together, but their hearts are divided) (59:14).

Allah ﷻ is telling us directly: no amount of money will fix this. No amount of programs, conferences, or interfaith work between our own ethnic groups will solve this. It is only Allah ﷻ and His word that can truly unite hearts.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّهُۥ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (Indeed He is the Almighty, the All-Wise). عَزِيزٌ means He has ultimate authority and power—He can do what no human effort can accomplish. حَكِيمٌ means He is All-Wise—He knows exactly how to bring about this transformation in the most perfect way.

One of the most powerful tools for breaking down racism and tribal barriers is language—specifically, the Arabic language. Not because of Arab supremacy, but because of Arabic supremacy—the language of the Quran has a special status that Allah ﷻ gave it.

When Muslims from different backgrounds share the language of the Quran, something beautiful happens. The barriers start to break down. The love for that language creates a connection, a bond between believers that transcends ethnicity.

Language is an Amanah (trust) that Allah ﷻ gave to those born into Arabic-speaking families. To honor that gift, Arabic should be something that unites the Ummah, not divides it. And for non-Arab Muslims, learning Arabic—even conversationally—creates bridges of understanding and love.

Our everyday language should be filled with Quranic expressions, reminding each other of Allah ﷻ in our regular conversations. This is very possible here, in this land where our children can pick up Spanish from a few television shows. If they can learn other languages so easily, they can certainly learn the language of their Creator.

May Allah ﷻ make all of us and our children at least bilingual—fluent in English and fluent in Arabic—as a step toward truly uniting our hearts the way He united the hearts of the early believers.

Ayah 68–75 · linked video: 10. Al-’Anfal (Ayah 68-75)

Surah Al-Anfal: Ayahs 68-75

Ayah 68

لَّوْلَا كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ سَبَقَ لَمَسَّكُمْ فِيمَآ أَخَذْتُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

Had not a decree from Allah ﷻ preceded, you would have been touched by a great punishment for what you took.

Before examining this ayah, we must understand a detail often overlooked. Allah ﷻ had already revealed guidance regarding prisoners of war before the Battle of Badr. In Surah Muhammad, the fourth ayah addresses the policy of dealing with captives after battle: when you meet those who disbelieve, strike their necks, but then the ayah includes an opening—“until you have made a great slaughter among them, then bind them firmly. Thereafter is the time for either generosity or ransom.” This phrase offered two possibilities: showing mercy to captives or accepting ransom for their release.

The Prophet ﷺ chose the more lenient option from this revelation, accepting ransoms and releasing prisoners. Allah ﷻ now clarifies that both approaches were legitimate within Islamic law. The severe approach would have been to execute the prisoners, while the lenient approach allowed their release. Had Allah ﷻ not left this opening for mercy in Surah Muhammad, accepting ransom would have violated divine law and brought severe consequences.

This situation reveals something profound about prophetic decision-making. The Messenger ﷺ can have multiple legitimate options before him, all falling within Islamic law. He works with the revelation given to him and exercises his own judgment. Out of reverence, we sometimes speak as though the Prophet ﷺ only acted upon revelation and had no personality of his own. But Allah ﷻ chose not just the most honest person on earth—He chose the most intelligent human being. That intelligence operates in making decisions, and only Allah’s direct revelation supersedes it. No human being could have corrected the Prophet’s judgment except Allah ﷻ Himself.

We must also understand the human complexity of this situation. These weren’t simply enemy combatants—some were closet Muslims too afraid to reveal their faith in Makkah, who had been pressured into joining the Makkan army. Some scholars mention that Abbas (RA), the Prophet’s uncle, had already embraced Islam internally but maintained appearances with his tribe. He came to Badr reluctantly and ended up captured as a prisoner of war. Abu al-As, the husband of the Prophet’s daughter Zainab (RA), was also among the captives.

During the night, the Prophet ﷺ heard Abbas (RA) groaning in pain because his bonds were too tight. He commanded the companions to loosen everyone’s bonds—not just Abbas, but everyone, to maintain fairness. Then Zainab (RA) sent her mother Khadijah’s necklace as ransom for her husband. When the Prophet ﷺ saw this necklace—one he had known for twenty-five years of his life—his entire past flashed before him. Overcome with emotion, he asked the Muslim community if they would permit him to return the necklace to his daughter without accepting it as ransom. He asked permission because the law is the law.

These were intensely personal situations—your daughter in distress, your uncle captured. In the midst of all this complexity, the Messenger ﷺ chose the lighter option. And now Allah ﷻ makes clear: there is no guilt in this choice.

Ayah 69

فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

So consume what you have taken of war booty as being lawful and good, and be conscious of Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful.

After clarifying that the lenient approach was permissible, Allah ﷻ now gives explicit permission: consume from what you have gained as war booty—it is lawful and pure. Maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Ghafur (Exceedingly Forgiving) and Rahim (Always Merciful).

This addresses any lingering guilt or hesitation. The decision has been made, divine approval has been given, and the matter is closed. Move forward without burden.

Ayah 70

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّمَن فِىٓ أَيْدِيكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَسْرَىٰٓ إِن يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ خَيْرًا يُؤْتِكُمْ خَيْرًا مِّمَّآ أُخِذَ مِنكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

O Prophet, say to whoever is in your hands of the captives, “If Allah ﷻ knows any good in your hearts, He will give you something better than what was taken from you, and He will forgive you. And Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful.”

Among the prisoners, there may have been those already interested in Islam, or even those who had secretly accepted Islam but were forced to join the Makkan army. However, you cannot show them special treatment based on what might be in their hearts—that would be unjust. Everyone must be treated equally under the law, regardless of internal states that only Allah ﷻ knows.

But for those who do have good intentions, Allah ﷻ provides a message of hope: “Tell whoever is in your possession among the prisoners of war—if Allah ﷻ knows of any good in your hearts, He will grant you better than what was taken from you.”

This creates a crucial distinction. Even those who came unwillingly, even those who may have good in their hearts, must still pay ransom. The law applies equally to all. They can return to Makkah after paying, and later make hijrah properly and join the Muslim community. But Allah ﷻ promises that whatever ransom is taken from them, if they truly have good in their hearts, He will return it to them and give them even better.

Then comes the beautiful phrase: “And He will forgive you.” Your relationship with Allah ﷻ is one matter. How Islamic law applies to you in this situation is another matter entirely. Don’t confuse the two. Just because the Muslims cannot show you leniency and must apply the law doesn’t mean Allah ﷻ won’t forgive you. Those are separate issues. Let the matter of your heart remain between you and Allah ﷻ. The matter of law between you and the Muslim community is a different domain.

“And Allah ﷻ is Ghafur (Exceedingly Forgiving), Rahim (Always Merciful).” We Muslims must abide by law and cannot make exceptions based on hidden intentions. But Allah ﷻ operates on a different plane—He sees what we cannot and forgives in ways we cannot.

Ayah 71

وَإِن يُرِيدُوا۟ خِيَانَتَكَ فَقَدْ خَانُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ فَأَمْكَنَ مِنْهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

But if they intend to betray you—then they have already betrayed Allah ﷻ before, and He empowered you over them. And Allah ﷻ is Knowing and Wise.

There’s always the possibility of deception. Some prisoners might claim belief, saying they were forced to come and fight, hoping for special treatment. Allah ﷻ addresses this: “If they intend to deceive you”—claiming faith falsely—“then they have already betrayed Allah ﷻ before.” The phrase “He empowered you over them” (amkana minhum) implies that Allah ﷻ enabled you to capture them in the first place. Their capture itself is part of divine wisdom.

“And Allah ﷻ is ’Alim (All-Knowing), Hakim (Ultimately Wise).” He knows their true intentions and His wisdom operates in how these situations unfold. Trust in that knowledge and wisdom.

Ayah 72

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَمْ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ مَا لَكُم مِّن وَلَـٰيَتِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ يُهَاجِرُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنِ ٱسْتَنصَرُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ فَعَلَيْكُمُ ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍۭ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُم مِّيثَـٰقٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

Indeed, those who have believed and emigrated and fought with their wealth and lives in the cause of Allah ﷻ and those who gave shelter and aided—those are allies of one another. But those who believed and did not emigrate—for you there is no obligation of protection toward them until they emigrate. And if they seek help of you for the religion, then you must help them, except against a people between yourselves and whom is a treaty. And Allah ﷻ is Seeing of what you do.

Now the ayah establishes categories of allegiance and loyalty. Notice the grammatical structure: Allah ﷻ uses two separate connectors (الَّذِينَ) to create two distinct groups.

The first group: “Those who believed, emigrated, and struggled with their wealth and their lives in Allah’s path.” This describes the Muhajirun. It’s significant that Allah ﷻ mentions jihad with wealth before jihad with their selves—both forms of struggle matter. Before the Battle of Badr, the small military expeditions the Prophet ﷺ sent were all composed of Muhajirun. None included the Ansar. The first time the Ansar became militarily involved was at Badr itself. So Allah ﷻ describes those who had been believers, who migrated, and who had been doing jihad both before and after migration—the Muhajirun.

The second group: “Those who provided shelter and aided.” The word آوَوْا (awaw) means to provide protection and refuge—it comes from the same root as مَأْوَى (ma’wa), a place of refuge and perfect protection. These are the Ansar of Madinah who opened their homes, shared their wealth, and supported the Muhajirun.

If this were one unified group, the ayah could have simply continued with conjunctions. But by using two separate definite articles, Allah ﷻ makes them two distinct groups with their own characteristics and contributions.

“Those—all of them—are protective friends and allies of one another.” The word أَوْلِيَاء indicates the deepest form of loyalty and protective friendship. Both groups, though distinct, have ultimate loyalty to each other.

Then comes a challenging statement: “And those who believed but did not emigrate—you owe no loyalty to them at all until they make hijrah.”

There were people in Makkah who claimed faith but refused to make the sacrifice of hijrah. Perhaps they turned back from Islam. Perhaps they weren’t willing to make the necessary sacrifice. The ayah establishes that you owe them nothing. Even if they call themselves Muslims, you are not to trust them in any way—not until they make hijrah themselves.

But there’s an important exception. Among those who didn’t migrate, there are two categories: those who didn’t want to migrate, and those who wanted to but couldn’t. Perhaps they were caught, captured, held back by family, lacked resources, or faced other obstacles. The ayah separates these two groups.

“However, if they seek your help in matters of religion, then helping them becomes obligatory upon you.” If someone in Makkah who wanted to migrate but couldn’t now asks for help—perhaps they want to escape but need assistance—then you must help them. You must come to their aid.

“Except against a people between whom and you there is a treaty.” If helping them would require military action against a group with whom you have a peace agreement, you cannot violate that treaty. Help means going militarily to extract them, like a rescue mission. You will undertake no military activity against any tribe or group with whom you have an existing agreement.

“And Allah ﷻ is Basir (fully seeing) of what you do.”

These ethics of war are revealed at the very moment of war itself. This is remarkable. When war happens, rules typically go out the window. There’s an expression: “All is fair in love and war.” Practically speaking, everything becomes lawless. There are no Geneva Conventions on the battlefield. When anger takes over, entire villages can be annihilated without a second thought.

A soldier on the battlefield, especially when alone or with close comrades away from oversight, fears only two things: that one of his own will report him, or that a superior might see. That’s his only restraint. Soldiers don’t follow rules because they love the rules—they often hate the restrictions. Whenever they believe the cameras aren’t watching, they do what they want. This is true across the board, regardless of nationality. There are noble soldiers occasionally, but generally, when human beings receive an open license to do whatever they want, they do exactly that.

The Muslim soldier operates from an entirely different framework. He knows Allah ﷻ is watching him constantly, at all times, in everything he does. If he’s conscious of that reality, he cannot act as other soldiers act. He cannot simply exact revenge when no superior officers are around. There are limits on him—internal limits. He doesn’t think, “No one’s watching, so I can do whatever I want.” The word of Allah ﷻ isn’t like a constitution or military code sitting on a shelf somewhere. It’s the word of Allah ﷻ being recorded by angels, with Allah ﷻ Himself watching at all times.

This phrase “And Allah ﷻ is Seeing of what you do” is strategically placed. Other soldiers don’t have that sense of authority over them. The Muslim soldier cannot escape divine oversight.

Ayah 73

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ إِلَّا تَفْعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتْنَةٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَفَسَادٌ كَبِيرٌ

And those who disbelieved are allies of one another. If you do not do this, there will be fitnah on earth and great corruption.

Just as the believers have formed alliances and bonds of loyalty, “those who disbelieve are also protective allies and loyal to one another.”

Then comes the warning: “If you do not do this”—if you don’t come to the aid of your people when they ask for help—“there will be great trial and tribulation in the land, and huge corruption will spread.”

What is this corruption? Muslims who were weak, who couldn’t make hijrah, will receive no help. What happens to them? They’ll abandon Islam, saying, “Why should we remain Muslim? They didn’t come to help us.” You’ll have a population of people who tasted Islam and then deliberately left it. That is enormous fitnah.

There are heartbreaking historical examples of this. In contemporary conflicts between Muslim groups—where political situations are complex and taking sides is difficult—there have been cases where Muslims were killed or abandoned, where Muslim women were captured by armies and assimilated into other cultures. In some cases they were converted to other faiths. Later, movements arose to bring these women back, but many refused. “Where were you when we needed rescuing? What are we supposed to do with you now? We have families, children. We’ve moved on.” That loss represents massive corruption—exactly what this ayah warns against.

Ayah 74

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاوَوا۟ وَّنَصَرُوٓا۟ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ حَقًّا ۚ لَّهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ

And those who have believed and emigrated and fought in the cause of Allah ﷻ and those who gave shelter and aided—it is they who are the believers, truly. For them is forgiveness and noble provision.

Allah ﷻ returns to describing the two groups: those who believed, migrated and struggled in Allah’s path (the Muhajirun), and those who provided shelter and help (the Ansar).

“Those are the true believers. Actually, those are the real believers.”
